title: Introduction to RapidFuzz
text: RapidFuzz provides high-performance string similarity distance functions powered C++ library rapidfuzz-cpp. useful tasks record linkage, fuzzy matching, typo correction, deduplication. vignette demonstrates main features package using data readily available R.

title: Introduction to RapidFuzz
text: Levenshtein distance counts minimum number single-character edits (insertions, deletions, substitutions) needed transform one string another.

title: Introduction to RapidFuzz
text: Let’s compare different metrics score pair strings:
code: s1 <- "California" s2 <- "Kalifornia"  data.frame(   metric = c("Levenshtein", "Damerau-Levenshtein", "Hamming", "Jaro",               "Jaro-Winkler", "LCSseq", "OSA", "Indel"),   distance = c(     levenshtein_distance(s1, s2),     damerau_levenshtein_distance(s1, s2),     hamming_distance(s1, s2),     round(jaro_distance(s1, s2), 4),     round(jaro_winkler_distance(s1, s2), 4),     lcs_seq_distance(s1, s2),     osa_distance(s1, s2),     indel_distance(s1, s2)   ),   normalized_similarity = c(     round(levenshtein_normalized_similarity(s1, s2), 4),     round(damerau_levenshtein_normalized_similarity(s1, s2), 4),     round(hamming_normalized_similarity(s1, s2), 4),     round(jaro_normalized_similarity(s1, s2), 4),     round(jaro_winkler_normalized_similarity(s1, s2), 4),     round(lcs_seq_normalized_similarity(s1, s2), 4),     round(osa_normalized_similarity(s1, s2), 4),     round(indel_normalized_similarity(s1, s2), 4)   ) ) #>                metric distance normalized_similarity #> 1         Levenshtein   1.0000                0.9000 #> 2 Damerau-Levenshtein   1.0000                0.9000 #> 3             Hamming   1.0000                0.9000 #> 4                Jaro   0.0667                0.9333 #> 5        Jaro-Winkler   0.0667                0.9333 #> 6              LCSseq   1.0000                0.9000 #> 7                 OSA   1.0000                0.9000 #> 8               Indel   2.0000                0.9000

title: Introduction to RapidFuzz
text: fuzz_* family functions provides different strategies comparing strings, especially useful word order partial matches matter.
code: # Exact content, different case/spacing fuzz_ratio("New York City", "new york city") #> [1] 76.92308  # Partial match: one string is contained in the other fuzz_partial_ratio("York", "New York City") #> [1] 100  # Word order doesn't matter fuzz_token_sort_ratio("City of New York", "New York City") #> [1] 89.65517  # Common tokens fuzz_token_set_ratio("New York City NY", "New York City") #> [1] 100  # Weighted ratio (best overall heuristic) fuzz_WRatio("New York City", "new york city!!") #> [1] 71.42857

what: New in v1.1.0: Partial Token Ratios
title: Introduction to RapidFuzz
text: combine benefits token-based comparison partial matching:
code: fuzz_partial_token_sort_ratio("Museum of Modern Art", "Modern Art Museum NYC") #> [1] 94.73684 fuzz_partial_token_set_ratio("Museum of Modern Art", "Modern Art Museum NYC") #> [1] 100 fuzz_partial_token_ratio("Museum of Modern Art", "Modern Art Museum NYC") #> [1] 100

what: 3. Matching Against a List of Choices
title: Introduction to RapidFuzz
text: common task finding best match query within list options. RapidFuzz provides three extract functions .

previous_headings: 3. Matching Against a List of Choices
title: Introduction to RapidFuzz
code: # Misspelled state names queries <- c("Kalifornia", "Nwe York", "Texs", "Florda", "Pensylvania") states <- state.name  # Find the best match for each misspelled name results <- lapply(queries, function(q) {   best <- extract_best_match(q, states, score_cutoff = 0)   data.frame(     query = q,     best_match = best$choice,     score = round(best$score, 2)   ) })  do.call(rbind, results) #>         query   best_match score #> 1  Kalifornia   California 90.00 #> 2    Nwe York     New York 87.50 #> 3        Texs        Texas 88.89 #> 4      Florda      Florida 92.31 #> 5 Pensylvania Pennsylvania 95.65

previous_headings: 3. Matching Against a List of Choices
title: Introduction to RapidFuzz
code: # Find top 5 states similar to "New" extract_matches("New", states, score_cutoff = 50, limit = 5, scorer = "PartialRatio") #>          choice score #> 1 New Hampshire   100 #> 2    New Jersey   100 #> 3    New Mexico   100 #> 4      New York   100 #> 5         Maine    80

previous_headings: 3. Matching Against a List of Choices
what: Extract All Matches Above a Threshold
title: Introduction to RapidFuzz
code: # All states with > 70% similarity to "North" extract_similar_strings("North", states, score_cutoff = 70) #>           choice score #> 1 North Carolina    90 #> 2   North Dakota    90

title: Introduction to RapidFuzz
text: extract_matches() function supports 10 different scorers. best choice depends data:
code: query <- "san francisco" cities <- c("San Francisco", "San Fernando", "Santa Fe", "San Diego",             "Francisco", "South San Francisco", "San Fran")  scorers <- c("Ratio", "PartialRatio", "TokenSortRatio", "TokenSetRatio",              "WRatio", "QRatio", "PartialTokenSortRatio",              "PartialTokenSetRatio", "PartialTokenRatio", "TokenRatio")  results <- lapply(scorers, function(sc) {   m <- extract_matches(query, cities, score_cutoff = 0, limit = 3, scorer = sc)   data.frame(scorer = sc, rank1 = m$choice[1], score1 = round(m$score[1], 1)) })  do.call(rbind, results) #>                   scorer         rank1 score1 #> 1                  Ratio San Francisco    100 #> 2           PartialRatio San Francisco    100 #> 3         TokenSortRatio San Francisco    100 #> 4          TokenSetRatio San Francisco    100 #> 5                 WRatio San Francisco    100 #> 6                 QRatio San Francisco    100 #> 7  PartialTokenSortRatio San Francisco    100 #> 8   PartialTokenSetRatio San Francisco    100 #> 9      PartialTokenRatio San Francisco    100 #> 10            TokenRatio San Francisco    100

title: Introduction to RapidFuzz
text: processString() function helps normalize strings comparison: especially useful matching names accented characters:
code: # Trim + lowercase processString("  São Paulo  ", processor = TRUE, asciify = FALSE) #> [1] "são paulo"  # Trim + lowercase + ASCII transliteration processString("  São Paulo  ", processor = TRUE, asciify = TRUE) #> [1] "sao paulo"  # ASCII only processString("Ñoño", processor = FALSE, asciify = TRUE) #> [1] "Nono" # Without preprocessing fuzz_ratio("São Paulo", "sao paulo") #> [1] 63.15789  # With preprocessing fuzz_ratio(   processString("São Paulo", processor = TRUE, asciify = TRUE),   processString("sao paulo", processor = TRUE, asciify = TRUE) ) #> [1] 100

title: Introduction to RapidFuzz
text: Edit operations show exactly transformations needed convert one string another.
code: # Levenshtein edit operations ops <- get_editops("saturday", "sunday") ops #>   src_pos dest_pos    type #> 1       1        1  delete #> 2       2        1  delete #> 3       4        2 replace # Apply the operations editops_apply_str(ops, "saturday", "sunday") #> [1] "saturday" # LCSseq edit operations lcs_seq_editops("kitten", "sitting") #>   operation source_position destination_position #> 1    insert               0                    0 #> 2    delete               0                    1 #> 3    insert               4                    4 #> 4    delete               4                    5 #> 5    insert               6                    6

title: Introduction to RapidFuzz
text: Useful comparing strings share beginnings endings:
code: # Same prefix "inter" prefix_similarity("international", "internet") #> [1] 6 prefix_normalized_similarity("international", "internet") #> [1] 0.4615385  # Same postfix "tion" postfix_similarity("education", "formation") #> [1] 5 postfix_normalized_similarity("education", "formation") #> [1] 0.5555556

title: Introduction to RapidFuzz
text: real-world scenario: matching messy data clean reference list.
code: # Simulated "dirty" records dirty <- c("J. Smith", "Jane M. Doe", "Bob Johnson Jr",             "Alice Wonderland", "Charlie Browne")  # Clean reference list clean <- c("John Smith", "Jane Mary Doe", "Robert Johnson Junior",            "Alice Wonder", "Charles Brown", "David Lee")  # Match each dirty record to the best clean record matches <- lapply(dirty, function(d) {   best <- extract_best_match(d, clean, score_cutoff = 0)   data.frame(     dirty_record = d,     matched_to = best$choice,     confidence = round(best$score, 1)   ) })  do.call(rbind, matches) #>       dirty_record            matched_to confidence #> 1         J. Smith            John Smith       77.8 #> 2      Jane M. Doe         Jane Mary Doe       83.3 #> 3   Bob Johnson Jr Robert Johnson Junior       85.5 #> 4 Alice Wonderland          Alice Wonder       85.7 #> 5   Charlie Browne         Charles Brown       88.9

title: Introduction to RapidFuzz
text: RapidFuzz implemented C++ significantly faster pure R alternatives string matching tasks.
code: # Compare performance: RapidFuzz vs base R adist s1 <- paste(sample(letters, 100, replace = TRUE), collapse = "") s2 <- paste(sample(letters, 100, replace = TRUE), collapse = "")  bench <- system.time(   for (i in 1:1000) levenshtein_distance(s1, s2) )  bench_base <- system.time(   for (i in 1:1000) adist(s1, s2) )  data.frame(   method = c("RapidFuzz", "base::adist"),   time_1000_calls = c(bench["elapsed"], bench_base["elapsed"]) ) #>        method time_1000_calls #> 1   RapidFuzz           0.003 #> 2 base::adist           0.036

title: String Similarity Computation Using RapidFuzz
text: Provides high-performance interface calculating string similarities distances, leveraging efficient C++ library RapidFuzz (v3.3.3) developed Max Bachmann Adam Cohen. package integrates C++ implementation, allowing R users access cutting-edge algorithms fuzzy matching text analysis.

title: String Similarity Computation Using RapidFuzz
text: can install directly CRAN development version GitHub :

title: String Similarity Computation Using RapidFuzz
text: RapidFuzz package R wrapper around highly efficient RapidFuzz C++ library. provides implementations multiple string comparison similarity metrics, Levenshtein, Jaro-Winkler, Damerau-Levenshtein distances. package particularly useful applications like record linkage, approximate string matching, fuzzy text processing. String comparison algorithms calculate distances similarities two sequences characters. distances help quantify similar two strings . example, Levenshtein distance measures minimum number single-character edits required transform one string another. RapidFuzz leverages advanced algorithms ensure high performance maintaining accuracy. original library open-source can accessed RapidFuzz GitHub Repository.

title: String Similarity Computation Using RapidFuzz
text: processString(): Process string options trim, convert lowercase, transliterate ASCII.

title: String Similarity Computation Using RapidFuzz
text: opcodes_apply_str(): Apply Opcodes transform string. opcodes_apply_vec(): Apply Opcodes transform string character vector.

title: String Similarity Computation Using RapidFuzz
text: get_editops(): Retrieve Edit Operations two strings.

title: String Similarity Computation Using RapidFuzz
text: editops_apply_str(): Apply Edit Operations transform string. editops_apply_vec(): Apply Edit Operations transform string character vector.

title: String Similarity Computation Using RapidFuzz
text: damerau_levenshtein_distance(): Calculate Damerau-Levenshtein Distance. damerau_levenshtein_normalized_distance(): Calculate Normalized Damerau-Levenshtein Distance. damerau_levenshtein_normalized_similarity(): Calculate Normalized Damerau-Levenshtein Similarity. damerau_levenshtein_similarity(): Calculate Damerau-Levenshtein Similarity.

title: String Similarity Computation Using RapidFuzz
text: fuzz_QRatio(): Perform Quick Ratio Calculation. fuzz_WRatio(): Perform Weighted Ratio Calculation. fuzz_partial_ratio(): Calculate Partial Ratio. fuzz_ratio(): Calculate Simple Ratio. fuzz_token_ratio(): Calculate Combined Token Ratio. fuzz_token_set_ratio(): Perform Token Set Ratio Calculation. fuzz_token_sort_ratio(): Perform Token Sort Ratio Calculation. fuzz_partial_token_sort_ratio(): Partial Token Sort Ratio (sorts words uses partial ratio). New v1.1.0 fuzz_partial_token_set_ratio(): Partial Token Set Ratio (token set + partial ratio). New v1.1.0 fuzz_partial_token_ratio(): Combined Partial Token Ratio (max partial token sort/set ratios). New v1.1.0

title: String Similarity Computation Using RapidFuzz
text: extract_similar_strings(): Find strings similarity threshold. extract_best_match(): Find best matching string set choices. extract_matches(): Extract top-N matches using configurable scorer.

title: String Similarity Computation Using RapidFuzz
text: hamming_distance(): Calculate Hamming Distance. hamming_normalized_distance(): Calculate Normalized Hamming Distance. hamming_normalized_similarity(): Calculate Normalized Hamming Similarity. hamming_similarity(): Calculate Hamming Similarity.

title: String Similarity Computation Using RapidFuzz
text: indel_distance(): Calculate Indel Distance. indel_normalized_distance(): Calculate Normalized Indel Distance. indel_normalized_similarity(): Calculate Normalized Indel Similarity. indel_similarity(): Calculate Indel Similarity.

title: String Similarity Computation Using RapidFuzz
text: jaro_distance(): Calculate Jaro Distance. jaro_normalized_distance(): Calculate Normalized Jaro Distance. jaro_normalized_similarity(): Calculate Normalized Jaro Similarity. jaro_similarity(): Calculate Jaro Similarity.

title: String Similarity Computation Using RapidFuzz
text: jaro_winkler_distance(): Calculate Jaro-Winkler Distance. jaro_winkler_normalized_distance(): Calculate Normalized Jaro-Winkler Distance. jaro_winkler_normalized_similarity(): Calculate Normalized Jaro-Winkler Similarity. jaro_winkler_similarity(): Calculate Jaro-Winkler Similarity.

title: String Similarity Computation Using RapidFuzz
text: lcs_seq_distance(): Calculate LCSseq Distance. lcs_seq_editops(): Retrieve LCSseq Edit Operations. lcs_seq_normalized_distance(): Calculate Normalized LCSseq Distance. lcs_seq_normalized_similarity(): Calculate Normalized LCSseq Similarity. lcs_seq_similarity(): Calculate LCSseq Similarity.

title: String Similarity Computation Using RapidFuzz
text: levenshtein_distance(): Calculate Levenshtein Distance. levenshtein_normalized_distance(): Calculate Normalized Levenshtein Distance. levenshtein_normalized_similarity(): Calculate Normalized Levenshtein Similarity. levenshtein_similarity(): Calculate Levenshtein Similarity.

title: String Similarity Computation Using RapidFuzz
text: osa_distance(): Calculate Distance Using OSA. osa_editops(): Retrieve Edit Operations Using OSA. osa_normalized_distance(): Calculate Normalized Distance Using OSA. osa_normalized_similarity(): Calculate Normalized Similarity Using OSA. osa_similarity(): Calculate Similarity Using OSA.

title: String Similarity Computation Using RapidFuzz
text: prefix_distance(): Calculate Prefix Distance two strings. prefix_normalized_distance(): Calculate Normalized Prefix Distance two strings. prefix_normalized_similarity(): Calculate Normalized Prefix Similarity two strings. prefix_similarity(): Calculate Prefix Similarity two strings.

title: String Similarity Computation Using RapidFuzz
text: postfix_distance(): Calculate Postfix Distance two strings. postfix_normalized_distance(): Calculate Normalized Postfix Distance two strings. postfix_normalized_similarity(): Calculate Normalized Postfix Similarity two strings. postfix_similarity(): Calculate Postfix Similarity two strings.

title: String Similarity Computation Using RapidFuzz
code: fuzz_partial_token_sort_ratio("fuzzy wuzzy was a bear", "wuzzy fuzzy was a bear") # Output: 100  fuzz_partial_token_set_ratio("fuzzy wuzzy was a bear", "fuzzy fuzzy was a bear") # Output: 100  fuzz_partial_token_ratio("fuzzy wuzzy was a bear", "wuzzy fuzzy was a bear") # Output: 100

title: String Similarity Computation Using RapidFuzz
code: # Example data query <- "new york jets" choices <- c("Atlanta Falcons", "New York Jets", "New York Giants", "Dallas Cowboys") score_cutoff <- 0.0  # Find the best match extract_matches(query, choices, score_cutoff, scorer = "PartialRatio") # Output: #            choice     score # 1   New York Jets 100.00000 # 2 New York Giants  81.81818 # 3 Atlanta Falcons  33.33333  # Using new scorers (v1.1.0) extract_matches(query, choices, score_cutoff, scorer = "PartialTokenRatio")

title: String Similarity Computation Using RapidFuzz
text: RapidFuzz package wrapper RapidFuzz C++ library (v3.3.3), developed Max Bachmann Adam Cohen. library implements efficient algorithms approximate string matching comparison.

title: Damerau-Levenshtein Distance — damerau_levenshtein_distance
text: Calculate Damerau-Levenshtein distance two strings. Computes Damerau-Levenshtein distance, edit distance allowing transpositions addition substitutions, insertions, deletions.

title: Damerau-Levenshtein Distance — damerau_levenshtein_distance
text: s1 string. first input string. s2 string. second input string. score_cutoff optional maximum threshold distance. Defaults largest integer value R (`.Machine$integer.max`).

title: Damerau-Levenshtein Distance — damerau_levenshtein_distance
text: Damerau-Levenshtein distance integer.

title: Normalized Damerau-Levenshtein Distance — damerau_levenshtein_normalized_distance
text: Calculate normalized Damerau-Levenshtein distance two strings. Computes normalized Damerau-Levenshtein distance, result 0.0 (identical) 1.0 (completely different).

title: Normalized Damerau-Levenshtein Distance — damerau_levenshtein_normalized_distance
text: s1 string. first input string. s2 string. second input string. score_cutoff optional maximum threshold normalized distance. Defaults 1.0.

title: Normalized Damerau-Levenshtein Distance — damerau_levenshtein_normalized_distance
text: normalized Damerau-Levenshtein distance double.

title: Normalized Damerau-Levenshtein Similarity — damerau_levenshtein_normalized_similarity
text: Calculate normalized Damerau-Levenshtein similarity two strings. Computes normalized similarity based Damerau-Levenshtein metric, result 0.0 (completely different) 1.0 (identical).

title: Normalized Damerau-Levenshtein Similarity — damerau_levenshtein_normalized_similarity
text: s1 string. first input string. s2 string. second input string. score_cutoff optional minimum threshold normalized similarity. Defaults 0.0.

title: Normalized Damerau-Levenshtein Similarity — damerau_levenshtein_normalized_similarity
text: normalized Damerau-Levenshtein similarity double.

title: Damerau-Levenshtein Similarity — damerau_levenshtein_similarity
text: Calculate Damerau-Levenshtein similarity two strings. Computes similarity based Damerau-Levenshtein metric, considers transpositions addition substitutions, insertions, deletions.

title: Damerau-Levenshtein Similarity — damerau_levenshtein_similarity
text: s1 string. first input string. s2 string. second input string. score_cutoff optional minimum threshold similarity score. Defaults 0.

title: Damerau-Levenshtein Similarity — damerau_levenshtein_similarity
text: Damerau-Levenshtein similarity integer.

title: Extract Best Match — extract_best_match
text: Compares query string strings list choices returns best match similarity score score_cutoff.

title: Extract Best Match — extract_best_match
text: query query string compare. choices vector strings compare query. score_cutoff numeric value specifying minimum similarity score (default 50.0). processor boolean indicating whether preprocess strings comparison (default TRUE).

what: Extract Matches with Scoring and Limit — extract_matches
title: Extract Matches with Scoring and Limit — extract_matches

title: Extract Matches with Scoring and Limit — extract_matches
code: extract_matches(   query,   choices,   score_cutoff = 50,   limit = 3L,   processor = TRUE,   scorer = "WRatio" )

title: Extract Matches with Scoring and Limit — extract_matches
text: query query string compare. choices vector strings compare query. score_cutoff numeric value specifying minimum similarity score (default 50.0). limit maximum number matches return (default 3). processor boolean indicating whether preprocess strings comparison (default TRUE). scorer string specifying similarity scoring method ("WRatio", "Ratio", "PartialRatio", etc.).

title: Extract Matches with Scoring and Limit — extract_matches
text: data frame containing top matched strings similarity scores.

title: Extract Matches — extract_similar_strings
text: Compares query string strings list choices returns elements similarity score score_cutoff.

title: Extract Matches — extract_similar_strings
text: query query string compare. choices vector strings compare query. score_cutoff numeric value specifying minimum similarity score (default 50.0). processor boolean indicating whether preprocess strings comparison (default TRUE).

title: Extract Matches — extract_similar_strings
text: data frame containing matched strings similarity scores.

Process a String — processString
text: Processes given input string applying optional trimming, case conversion, ASCII transliteration.

title: Process a String — processString
text: input std::string representing input string processed. processor bool indicating whether trim whitespace convert string lowercase. Default true. asciify bool indicating whether transliterate non-ASCII characters closest ASCII equivalents. Default false.

title: Process a String — processString
text: std::string representing processed string.

title: Process a String — processString
text: function applies following transformations input string, order: Trimming (processor = TRUE): Removes leading trailing whitespace. Lowercasing (processor = TRUE): Converts characters lowercase. ASCII Transliteration (asciify = TRUE): Replaces accented special characters closest ASCII equivalents.

title: Process a String — processString
code: # Example usage processString("  Éxâmple!  ", processor = TRUE, asciify = TRUE) #> [1] "example!" # Returns: "example!"  processString("  Éxâmple!  ", processor = TRUE, asciify = FALSE) #> [1] "éxâmple!" # Returns: "éxâmple!"  processString("  Éxâmple!  ", processor = FALSE, asciify = TRUE) #> [1] "  Example!  " # Returns: "Éxâmple!"  processString("  Éxâmple!  ", processor = FALSE, asciify = FALSE) #> [1] "  Éxâmple!  " # Returns: "  Éxâmple!  "

title: RapidFuzz 1.1.0
text: Fixed WRatio length ratio exactly 8.0. Added C++11 C++14 compatibility upstream library. Improved calculation minimum score inside partial_ratio skip alignments. Fixed incorrect score calculation SIMD implementations Levenshtein OSA 32-bit systems. Banded implementation LCS/Indel faster runtime score_cutoff.

title: RapidFuzz 1.1.0
text: fuzz_partial_token_sort_ratio(): combines token sorting partial ratio matching. fuzz_partial_token_set_ratio(): combines token set comparison partial ratio matching. fuzz_partial_token_ratio(): returns maximum partial_token_set_ratio partial_token_sort_ratio. extract_matches() now supports additional scorers: "TokenSortRatio", "TokenSetRatio", "TokenRatio", "QRatio", "PartialTokenSortRatio", "PartialTokenSetRatio", "PartialTokenRatio", addition existing "WRatio", "Ratio", "PartialRatio".

title: RapidFuzz 1.1.0
text: Added cli package integration formatted startup messages. Updated package documentation website.

title: RapidFuzz 1.1.0
text: Added Marcos Wasilew Carlos Amorin package authors.

title: RapidFuzz 1.0
text: CRAN release: 2024-12-05 Initial CRAN submission.
